Much more concentrated; dilute with water
Much more liquid; reduce other liquids
Strain yogurt for a thicker result
Salt and drain to remove bitterness
Einkorn is denser and nuttier; use 1:1 for a heritage result.
Very different flavor profile; use only as a thickener alternative.

groups.name: Sarma / Dolma
groups.description: Stuffed and rolled dishes spanning from the Ottoman heartland to Scandinavia. Grape leaves, cabbage, and collard greens wrap rice and meat fillings across at least six distinct regional traditions, each claiming the dish as its own.
recipe_members: Aegean Olive Oil Stuffed Grape Leaves
Lebanese Stuffed Grape Leaves with Lamb
story_members: Rolling Sarma by the Black Sea
From Anatolia to Athens: The Dolma Trail
How Swedish Cabbage Rolls Came from the Ottoman Empire

journey_steps.story: Nomadic Turkic peoples wrapped seasoned meat in leaves for portable meals during long migrations across the steppe, carrying the technique westward as they moved.
As Turkic groups settled in Anatolia, grape leaves from local vineyards replaced steppe grasses, and rice from Persian trade routes enriched the filling. The dish took root in every household.
Palace kitchens in Istanbul refined sarma into dozens of regional variations. The dish spread with the Empire to the Balkans, Levant, and North Africa, each region adapting it to local produce.
Greek dolmadakia emerged with lemon-egg sauce, while Balkan versions favoured sauerkraut leaves and smoked meat. Each culture embraced the technique as its own ancient tradition.
Lebanese and Syrian cooks added pomegranate molasses and spiced lamb, creating a distinctly Levantine branch noted for its tartness and deep colour.

King Charles XII returned from Ottoman exile in 1714 bringing the concept of stuffed cabbage leaves. Kåldolmar became a Swedish staple, served with lingonberry jam — a culinary bridge between two distant empires.

cultural_facts.text: The word 'dolma' derives from the Turkish verb 'doldurmak', meaning 'to stuff'. 'Sarma' comes from 'sarmak', meaning 'to wrap' — two verbs that define an entire culinary tradition.
Swedish kåldolmar trace directly to Charles XII's Ottoman exile of 1709–1714, making them one of the clearest documented examples of culinary transfer between the Ottoman Empire and Northern Europe.
In many Black Sea households, sarma-rolling is a communal activity: neighbours gather the day before a wedding to prepare hundreds of rolls together, turning the kitchen into a social institution.

groups.name: Dumplings of the Silk Road
groups.description: From Turkish manti to Chinese xiaolongbao, filled dough parcels trace the ancient trade routes connecting East and West. Each culture along the Silk Road developed its own pocket of dough and filling, yet the underlying technique is unmistakably shared.
story_members: The Silk Road: How Noodles Travelled the World

journey_steps.story: Jiaozi and early filled dumplings emerged in northern China as a way to seal nourishing fillings inside thin wheat dough during harsh winters. The form spread rapidly across the empire.
Traders carried the dumpling concept westward through the mountain passes. Samarkand's bazaars became a crossroads where Chinese, Persian, and Turkic filling traditions merged into samsa and manti.

Migrating Turkic groups brought manti to Anatolia, where cooks miniaturised the dumplings to extreme smallness — a point of pride in Kayseri — and crowned them with garlic yogurt and red pepper butter.

Pierogi emerged in Poland and Ukraine, likely through eastern trade and migration. Potato and cheese fillings replaced meat as the region's signature variation, and the dumpling became embedded in Christmas Eve ritual.

cultural_facts.text: Turkish manti are traditionally so small that forty of them should fit on a single spoon, according to a saying from Kayseri — the city considered the spiritual home of the dish.
The Silk Road was not a single route but a network of trade paths spanning over 6,000 kilometres, along which culinary techniques travelled alongside silk, spices, and ideas about flavour.

groups.name: Fermented Traditions
groups.description: Fermentation connects cuisines across East Asia and beyond. From Korean kimchi to Japanese miso, these living foods reflect centuries of microbial partnership between humans and nature — patience transformed into flavour.
story_members: Kimjang: The Community Work of Kimchi

journey_steps.story: Early fermentation of soybeans and vegetables began in ancient China, producing the precursors of soy paste and pickled vegetables. The technique spread through trade and migration across East Asia.

Salted and fermented vegetables evolved into what would become kimchi. The communal practice of kimjang developed as communities prepared vast winter stores together, binding neighbourhoods through shared labour.

Buddhist monks brought soybean fermentation techniques from China. Japanese craftspeople refined the process into miso, developing regional varieties across the archipelago, from white Kyoto miso to rich red Sendai miso.

cultural_facts.text: Kimjang, the communal practice of making and sharing kimchi, was inscribed on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2013 — one of the few food preparation traditions to receive this recognition.

A single batch of miso can take anywhere from six months to three years to ferment. Longer-aged varieties develop deeper, more complex flavours — Japanese cooks say the miso is 'listening' to the seasons.

groups.name: Köfte
groups.description: Ground and shaped meat, seasoned with spices and herbs, cooked over fire. The principle behind köfte is ancient and nearly universal, yet the path from Central Asian nomadic camps to Ottoman palace kitchens to Swedish IKEA counters is surprisingly direct.
story_members: How Swedish Cabbage Rolls Came from the Ottoman Empire

journey_steps.story: Nomadic Turkic communities shaped and grilled ground meat over open fires, seasoning it with whatever wild herbs grew on the steppe. The portable, protein-rich form made it ideal for life on horseback.

Ottoman court and street kitchens developed dozens of regional köfte variations — from İnegöl köfte with its pure meat simplicity to Adana kebab rolled on skewers with red pepper. Every city claimed its own signature shape and spice blend.

King Charles XII tasted köfte during his Ottoman exile of 1709–1714 and brought the concept home. Swedish cooks replaced bulgur with breadcrumbs, added cream and allspice, and shaped the mixture into smaller balls. Köttbullar entered the national canon alongside kåldolmar.

cultural_facts.text: Turkish cuisine recognises over 290 regional köfte varieties, from the breadcrumb-free İnegöl köfte of Bursa to the raw çiğ köfte of Şanlıurfa — each city fiercely proud of its own version.

In 2018, Sweden's official Twitter account acknowledged that Swedish meatballs were based on a recipe Charles XII brought back from the Ottoman Empire — a tweet that delighted Turkey and surprised most Swedes.

cultural_events.name: Nevruz / Nowruz
cultural_events.description: Celebrated on the spring equinox (around 21 March), Nowruz is a 3,000-year-old new year tradition observed across Iran, Afghanistan, Central Asia, parts of Turkey, and diaspora communities worldwide. The haft-sin table, set with seven symbolic items beginning with the Persian letter 's', is the visual centrepiece, while sabzi polo mahi (herb rice with fish) is the canonical new year dish.

cultural_events.name: Hıdırellez
cultural_events.description: Festival marking the start of summer in Turkish and Balkan folk tradition. Celebrated on the night of 5–6 May, it commemorates the legendary meeting of the prophets Hızır and İlyas, who represent the abundance of land and sea. Communities gather outdoors, light bonfires, and share grilled meats.

cultural_events.name: Dongzhi Festival
cultural_events.description: The Dongzhi (冬至) winter solstice festival is one of the most important Chinese celebrations, when families gather to eat tangyuan (glutinous rice balls) in the south or dumplings in the north. The festival honours the return of longer days and the yang energy growing within the yin of winter.

cultural_events.name: Ramadan Iftar
cultural_events.description: The breaking of the daily fast during Ramadan is one of the most significant communal dining events in Islamic cultures worldwide. From Levantine iftar tables laden with grape leaves, fattoush, and lamb to Moroccan harira and Indonesian kolak, the iftar meal reflects each region's culinary identity within a shared spiritual framework.
linked_recipes: Lebanese Stuffed Grape Leaves with Lamb

cultural_events.name: Chuseok
cultural_events.description: Chuseok (추석) is the Korean harvest festival celebrated on the 15th day of the 8th lunar month. Families gather to honour ancestors with food offerings (charye), prepare songpyeon (half-moon rice cakes), and share hearty stews made with newly harvested ingredients.
